title: Handling Out-of-Vocabulary Classes
concept: Hierarchical classification handles unseen classes by falling back to confident parent nodes.

exercises.prompt: Recap: How do conditional probability chains prevent logical contradictions in hierarchical models?

options.text: By forcing the child probability to be mathematically bounded by its parent's probability.
By deleting low-probability nodes from memory.
By training the model on twice as many images.

exercises.explain: Correct! Because child probability is parent probability multiplied by conditional probability, it can never exceed parent probability.

cards.title: It's a bird of some kind
cards.body: Imagine walking through a zoo and seeing a strange, exotic bird you've never heard of. You don't have a label for it in your brain. But you don't say 'it's a car' or 'it's a banana'. You confidently say: 'That is some kind of bird'. Models can do this too.

cards.title: Semantic Fallback
cards.body: Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) handling in hierarchical models allows a classifier to stop making predictions down the tree when confidence drops below a threshold. Instead of guessing a specific child label, the model outputs a confident parent label (e.g., 'Bird' instead of guessing a specific species).

cards.title: A cautious student
cards.body: Think of a student taking a multiple choice exam. If they are unsure of the specific species of a plant, they write down 'Fern family' rather than guessing a specific genus and risking a massive penalty for a confidently wrong answer.

cards.title: Industrial sorting fallback
cards.body: A recycling robot sorts plastic items. It encounters a newly designed bottle it has never seen. Instead of misclassifying it as a 'milk jug', the model stops at the parent node and outputs 'Plastic Container', routing it to the correct recycling bin.

cards.title: Unseen does not mean unclassifiable
cards.body: You might think a model is completely useless if it encounters a class it wasn't trained on. In hierarchical setups, as long as the unseen class belongs to a known parent branch, the model can still provide highly useful general classifications.

cards.title: Fallback Decisions
cards.body: How the model stops predicting when confidence drops:

right.text:
No fallback available
Forces choice: 'Lion' vs. 'House Cat'
Guesses wrong confidently

exercises.prompt: What does a hierarchical classifier do when it is unsure of the specific sub-class but confident in the general category?
exercises.explain: Semantic fallback allows models to safely output a general parent category when child confidence is low.

options.text: It stops descending the tree and outputs a confident parent class label.
It throws a runtime error and crashes.
It outputs a random child class with 100% confidence.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An out-of-vocabulary class can still be partially classified correctly if the model identifies its parent category.
exercises.explain: True. Even if the model has never seen a specific breed, it can still label it as a 'Dog' if the parent node activates.
Semantic fallback is the strategy of retreating up the hierarchy tree to a safe, general parent category.
exercises.sentence: Outputting a general parent label when child confidence is low is called semantic ___.

exercises.prompt: Which of these model outputs shows semantic fallback in action?
exercises.explain: Choosing to output the confident parent category (Dog) instead of the low-confidence child (Poodle) is fallback.

exercises.prompt: You are building a wild animal alert system for hikers. If the model sees a rare, dangerous bear species that wasn't in the training set, how should you configure the hierarchical prediction?

options.text: Configure a confidence threshold on child nodes. If none pass, fall back to outputting the confident parent 'Bear', keeping hikers alert.
Force the model to predict 'Teddy Bear' as a default child class.
Disable the parent nodes so the model is forced to choose 'Wolf' or 'Cougar'.
Ignore the image if bear confidence is below 99%.

exercises.explain: Exactly! By falling back to 'Bear', hikers are still alerted to the danger, whereas a flat model might have guessed 'Dog' or 'Squirrel', leading to a dangerous situation. Hierarchical fallback is a powerful tool. Fantastic job completing the Hierarchical Classification unit!
